game_name: Heroes of Might and Magic II: The Succession Wars

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Players construct and upgrade castles, with one upgrade per turn allowing gradual progression and planning
Fixed special abilities per hero class, foundational to character identity
Securing resources and building stronger armies as foundational 4X mechanic
Heroes learn up to eight secondary skills (Wisdom, Logistics, etc.) progressing from Basic to Advanced to Expert levels, creating distinctiveness and strategic depth
Magic point system replaces memorization; spells vary in cost, maintaining balance and strategic choice
Certain army units can be upgraded with improved statistics and abilities, adding progression layers
Players guide heroes to fight other heroes, requiring tactical decision-making
Core end-game objective: finding and defeating opponents through capturing their castles
Player chooses good (Knight, Sorceress, Wizard) or evil (Necromancer, Warlock, Barbarian) path, affecting available factions and campaign storyline
Victories in specific scenarios grant lasting bonuses that carry into future scenarios; players select starting bonuses based on strategy

typical_relief_triggers: Completing scenario objectives with bonus carryover
typical_tension_triggers: Hero combat encounters with uncertain outcomes
Scenario time pressure or escalating opposition
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based strategic pacing with gradual escalation; early game allows passive resource building and hero development, mid-game introduces combat encounters and faction opposition, late-game focuses on large-scale siege warfare and opposing hero confrontation. Tension builds through turn progression as enemy forces strengthen, relieved by successful captures and hero power growth.

difficulty_curve.early_game: Low pressure; players build castle infrastructure, recruit initial armies, and develop hero skills in protected or lightly-contested territory
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate pressure; introduction of secondary skills, magic systems, and initial combat encounters with opposing heroes; resource competition increases
difficulty_curve.late_game: High pressure; large-scale battles, multiple opposing forces, siege warfare, and endgame objective of capturing all enemy castles; hero and army combinations determine strategic outcomes
notable_spikes: First hero-vs-hero combat when prepared differently than expected
Facing multiple enemy heroes simultaneously requiring tactical priority
Late-game castle sieges requiring coordinated magic and army unit upgrades
Scenario transitions where carryover bonuses create new strategic asymmetries

notable_level_patterns.description: Multi-scenario campaigns where victories grant lasting bonuses that carry forward. Players select starting bonuses per scenario based on chosen strategy, creating emergent difficulty and strategic continuity across a series of maps.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Good vs Evil campaign structure (Roland's good campaign vs Archibald's evil campaign)

notable_level_patterns.description: Each campaign aligns with three factions (good: Knight/Sorceress/Wizard; evil: Necromancer/Warlock/Barbarian). Scenarios likely feature faction-specific units, spells, and town features, creating narrative coherence and tactical variety.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Any scenario in Roland's or Archibald's campaign

notable_level_patterns.description: Players secure resources and build stronger armies before attempting conflict, establishing a 4X loop of expansion, development, and conquest. Single upgrade per turn creates pacing and planning windows.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Early scenarios where unchallenged resource gathering precedes first major encounter
